coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots “CQDs” were investigated as photo-luminescent nanomaterials as it advantageous with nontoxicity to be alternative for metallic-nanomaterials in different purposes. Therefore, the presented report demonstrates an innovative strategy for industrialization of antimicrobial/fluorescent cotton textiles via exploitation of “CQDs”. Unique/novel infrared-assisted technique was currently investigated for clustering “CQDs” form carboxymethyl cellulose. The successive nucleation of “CQDs” (8.0 nm) was affirmed via infra-red, Raman spectroscopy, NMR, TEM and Zeta-potential analysis. The clustered “CQDs” showed antimicrobial and fluorescent characters. The minimal inhibition concentration for “CQDs” (100 mg/mL) against E. coli and C. albicans showed pathogenic reduction of 96% and 82%, respectively. Fluorescent emission spectra for “CQDs” showed two intense peaks at 415–445 nm. “CQDs” were loaded upon pristine and cationized cotton to prepare CQDs@cotton and CQDs@cationized cotton. While, their physical/mechanical properties (air and water vapor permeabilities, tensile strength and elongation %) and thermal stability (TGA & DTG analysis) were studied. The CQDs@cationized cotton exhibited excellent antimicrobial activity with good durability as after ten repretitive washings, inhibition zone diameter against E. coli, was diminished from 21.0 mm to 14.0 mm. The fluorescent emmision intensity was diminished from 741 to 287 after 10 washing cycles. The produced cotton fabrics could be safely used in the medical and military textiles.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots “CQDs” were investigated as photo-luminescent nanomaterials as it advantageous with nontoxicity to be alternative for metallic-nanomaterials in different purposes. Therefore, the presented report demonstrates an innovative strategy for industrialization of antimicrobial/fluorescent cotton textiles via exploitation of “CQDs”. Unique/novel infrared-assisted technique was currently investigated for clustering “CQDs” form carboxymethyl cellulose. The successive nucleation of “CQDs” (8.0 nm) was affirmed via infra-red, Raman spectroscopy, NMR, TEM and Zeta-potential analysis. The clustered “CQDs” showed antimicrobial and fluorescent characters. The minimal inhibition concentration for “CQDs” (100 mg/mL) against E. coli and C. albicans showed pathogenic reduction of 96% and 82%, respectively. Fluorescent emission spectra for “CQDs” showed two intense peaks at 415–445 nm. “CQDs” were loaded upon pristine and cationized cotton to prepare CQDs@cotton and CQDs@cationized cotton. While, their physical/mechanical properties (air and water vapor permeabilities, tensile strength and elongation %) and thermal stability (TGA & DTG analysis) were studied. The CQDs@cationized cotton exhibited excellent antimicrobial activity with good durability as after ten repretitive washings, inhibition zone diameter against E. coli, was diminished from 21.0 mm to 14.0 mm. The fluorescent emmision intensity was diminished from 741 to 287 after 10 washing cycles. The produced cotton fabrics could be safely used in the medical and military textiles.

ce:section.ce:para: Photo-luminescent nanostructures extensively attracted the scientific attention for their formidable applications in various purposes. For a long time, some of researchers focused on preparation and characterization for a type of semiconductor nanomaterials, ascribed with quantum dots “QDs”, owing to their excellent potentials rather than dyes, specially their characteristic emitted color that might be easily interchanged by controlling the particle size of the prepared nano-crystals [1–4]. Such mentioned “QDs” exhibited by limited applications specially in medical purposes as they often compose of toxin metallic compounds [5,6].

Therefore, researchers interested in this field of study started in looking for alternative photo-luminescent nanostructures with lower toxicity. Carbon quantum dots “CQD” recently investigated as a novel type of photo-luminescent nanomaterials as it superiorly advantageous with lack of toxicity which make it appropriate to be alternative for metallic-based “QDs” and organic dyes in different purposes such as electronic, sensors, and biological fields [7–10]. While, “CQDs” showed to exhibit numerous advantages such as high resistance to photo-bleaching, costless and simple synthesis processing, easy bio-conjugation.

“CQDs” are spherical carbon nanomaterials with main size of 1–10 nm, were characterized with temperature resistance, uv-resistance, corrosion resistance, superior thermal/electrical conductivity, high plasticity, high adsorption and excellent catalytic potentiality [11,12]. “CQDs” are ascribed to be like graphite sheet and are mainly constructed of sp2 carbons oriented in plane. Recent approaches were concerned in synthesizing of “CQDs” and their exploitation in various applications like manufacturing of super-capacitor, battery, biosensor, transistor, fuel cell, etc. [13–15]. However, according to literature, there is no researching approaches were considered with investigation the superiority of cellulose derivatives in generation of “CQDs”.

Since “CQDs” were investigated, numerous innovative techniques for synthesis of “CQDs” were reported [16–21]. Generally, all the synthetic methodologies of “CQDs” could be classified into two classes: top-down (arc discharge, laser ablation and acidic oxidation) and bottom up techniques (combustion routes, microwave pyrolysis, solvothermal/hydrothermal synthesis, electrochemical methodologies). As in the top-down technique, the macromolecular structures are depolymerized or suspended as small-sized “CQDs” by physical or chemical methodologies. While, in the bottom-up approaches, polymerization, carbonization and aromatization for a group of small molecules to generate the required “CQDs” is proceeded via certain chemical interaction. Moreover, microwave pyrolysis was reported to be one of the ultra-fast methodologies as one of the methods belonging to the bottom up class [22].

Carboxyl/hydroxyl containing carbon compounds that separately or concurrently with nitrogen-based molecules were commonly exploited for synthesis of “CQDs”, where, it was found that, the emitted color of “CQDs” could be tuned between bluish and greenish color by adjusting the ratios of mass for the original precursor [23–27]. For example, tuning the ratios of citrate and urea of moles as molecular precursors for “CQDs” from 3.0:1.0 to 6.0:1.0, 9.0:1.0, and 12.0:1.0 generated “CQDs” with emission bands detected at 450.0, 467.0, 536.0, and 526.0 nm, respectively [26]. The variation in emission bands was owing to the difference in the contents of “carbon and oxygen-rich surface groups”. According to our knowledge, Infra-red assisted synthetic technique for production of “CQDs” was never been reported in literature, while, infra-red synthetic assisted methodology could be advantageous with novelty, cheapness, simplicity and could be controllable and wide-scaled applicable in industry for manufacturing of “CQDs”.

With the progression in textile industrialization and technology, the demand of the functional textiles is greatly increased, in addition to the worldwide requirement of multifunctional textiles applicable in various purposes, such like, medical and healthcare, cosmetic textiles, fitness, sporting, UV-protection, camping and military [28,29]. The comfortability of the modified textiles is recognized as a key aspect of its design. In the last decade, various researching approaches were interested in the modification of textiles with multi-functions such as anti-insects [30,31], flame retardant & water repellent [32], self-cleaning [33], antimicrobial [34–38] and UV-protective actions [37,39,40]. The investigative studying in functionalization of textiles as masking materials, air-filtration matrices for protection from the toxic gas is mainly relied on the highly adsorptive properties of hydrophobic activated carbonic structures, while, the adsorptive action of these materials is not conflicting with playing as secondary emitters with their contamination [41].

In a comparison with metallic/nanometallic based composites, “CQDs” are characterized by its safer character which reflects their preference for medical applications. Herein, an innovative approach is represented for preparation of “CQDs” from carboxymethyl cellulose via unique/novel infrared-assisted synthetic route, that is never been reported. The synthesized “CQDs” were sequentially exploited in production of antimicrobial fluorescent cotton fabrics. The preparation of “CQDs” was confirmed via FTIR, Raman spectroscopy, 1HNMR, 13CNMR, TEM and Zeta-potential analysis. Consequently, the functionality of treated fabrics was investigated by FT-IR, SEM, EDX, XRD, photoluminescence and bactericidal potentiality. Moreover, their physical (air and water vapor permeability), mechanical properties and thermal stability were all studied for the modified fabrics. The durability of the acquired fluorescent and antimicrobial activities for the treated fabrics was monitored up to ten repetitive washing cycles.

Carboxymethyl cellulose (Mw ~ 250,000, Sigma-Aldrich), 3-chloro-2-hydroxypropyl trimethylammonium chloride (C6H15NOCl2, Sigma-Aldrich) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, Sigma-Aldrich) were applied as obtained without any purifications. Pretreated plain-woven 100% cotton (185 gm/m2) supplied from Weaving and Spinning Misr Company in El-Mehalla El-Kobra, Egypt.

“CQDs” were uniquely prepared from “CMC” via infra-red assisted synthetic route as follows; certain concentration of NaOH (10 g/l or 20 g/l) was used for hydrolyzing “CMC” (10 g/l or 20 g/l) and afterword the colloidal solution was magnetically stirred for two hours at 90 °C. Consequently, for complete clustering of “CQDs”, the colloidal solution was poured in vertical autoclave cup and exposed to infra-red irradiation (Infra-Red Color Dyeing Machine from India) for 6 h at 125 °C (with rate of 5 °C per minute). Finally, the darken brownish obtained colloid with was left in atmospheric air for cooling then turned to dialysis with distilled water via pur-A-lyzer dialysis kits overnight (MWCO 6–8 kDa from Sigma-Aldrich) to eliminate the enlarged particles and preserve the pure “CQDs” for further analyses. Table 1 represents the experimental conditions for preparation the different samples in the current approach.

Cationization of fabrics was performed using “3-chloro-2-hydroxypropyl tri-methyl ammonium chloride” as quaternary ammonium compound in two sequential steps that can be explained; as first step that can be described as activating step, cotton fabric was pre-activated with treatment in NaOH, as two grams of fabric were impregnated in one hundred milliliter of distilled H2O containing (0.49 g or 12.30 mmole/L) of NaOH, in 250 mL Erlenmeyer flask and then transferred for shaking for 6 h at ambient conditions. Consequently, the fabrics were washed with distilled H2O for removing of the unreacted NaOH. The activated cotton was exposed for drying in vacuum oven at 60 °C to be ready for further analyses.

The second step ascribed as cationization, the activated fabrics were treated with 2.1 g (12.3 mmole/L) of “3-chloro-2-hydroxypropyl tri-methyl ammonium chloride” as it was dissolved in two hundred and fifty milliliter Erlenmeyer flask containing 100 mL of distilled H2O and then 2 g of the activated cotton fabric were subsequently added. The prepared fabric was transferred for shacking in a water bath at 40 °C for 6 h. The cationized fabrics were filtered, then taken for washing with distilled H2O. Eventually, at 60 °C, the modified fabrics were dried under vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading of “CQDs” layers upon pristine and cationized cotton
ce:section.ce:para: Four repetitive layers (4L) of the ingrained “CQDs” were loaded upon the pristine and cationized cotton fabrics via soaking. Under continuous stirring, in 25 mL of “CQDs”, cotton fabrics (pieces of 0.25 g) were soaked for 1 h. Afterwards, before characterization, the treated cotton were air dried. The obtained fabrics were labeled as CQDs@cotton for native cotton treated with “CQDs” and CQDs@Q-cotton for cationized cotton treated with “CQDs”.

For instrumental characterization of “CQDs”, IR & NMR spectral analyses were detected by Perkin-Elmer 1600 FTIR (Perkin-Elmer, Waltham, MA, USA) discs, and Jeol-Ex-300 NMR spectrometer (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan), respectively. The chemical shifting was mentioned in ppm with TMS as a blank. 13C and 1H chemical shifts were assigned to solvent signal (DMSO) at 39.52 and 2.50 ppm, respectively. Results were represented as chemical shifting, integration, multiplicity (s = singlet, d = doublet) and coupling constants in Hertz (Hz). Raman spectra for the synthesized “CQDs” prior and post dialysis were detected using alpha300R – confocal Raman imaging (WITec from focus innovations – Germany). The spectral measurement was performed with diffraction-limited spatial resolution in the range of 500–3500 cm−1.

The absorption spectral data for “CMC” and the nucleated “CQDs” were detected at 250–750 nm via spectrophotometry “Cary 100 UV–VIS, UV–Vis-NIR Systems, from Agilent”. Geometrical properties and size distribution of the prepared “CQDs” were obtained from anticipation of “High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope from Japan JEOL-JEM-1200”. Size distribution of “CQDs” was estimated by “4 pi analysis software from USA” for minimum 50 particles. IR spectra were detected by “Jasco FT/IR 6100 spectrometer”, while, absorption spectra were shown at 500 – 4000 cm−1 using fifteen smoothing points, 4 cm−1 resolution, with scanning rate of 2 mm.sec -1 for 64 scanning times. Optical activities for the synthesized “CQDs” in UV–Visible range were monitored by spectro-florometer “JASCO FP8300”. The results were detected with exciting at 340 nm under ambient conditions.

The modified fabrics (CQDs@cotton and CQDs@Q-cotton) were characterized by high resolution scanning electron microscope (HRSEM Quanta FEG 250 with field emission gun, FEI Company – Netherlands). The elemental analyses were also detected by energy dispersive X-ray instrument (EDX AME-TEK analyzer). IR spectral data for the treated cotton (CQDs@cotton and CQDs@Q-cotton) were estimated by using “Jasco FT/IR 6100 spectrometer”. The absorbance spectral results were in range of 400–4000 cm−1 by 4 cm−1 resolution and in rate of two mm/sec for sixty four times of scanning. Additionally, the treated fabrics were analyzed by powder X-ray diffraction using X’Pert MPD diffractometer system from Philips, at room temperature. Diffraction patterns were detected in the diffraction angle (2θ) range of 3.5–50° using monochromatized (Cu Kα X-radiation at 40 kV, 50 mA and λ = 1.5406 Å).

Physical and mechanical characters of the modified fabrics were additionally investigated, while, tensile strength (MPa) and elongation at break (%) for the treated fabrics were estimated related to ASTM method D2256-66T by using the strip test methodology on the tester instrument (Asano machine MFG. Co. Ltd., Japan) [42]. Air permeability of the modified cotton fabrics were tested on Toyosiki (JIKA) apparatus according to ASTM D737-75 standard test method for air permeability of fabrics [43]. The tested area of the fabric was 6 cm diameter. Moreover, water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) as function of water vapor permeability was evaluated for the modified fabrics by using GBPI W303 (B) water vapor permeability (WVP) Analyzer from China in accordance with the standard methodology of ASTM E96 [44]. The measurements were carried out through the cup methodology under controllable conditions at temperature 38 °C and 4% humidity. WVTR was evaluated from the transferred amounts of water vapor (g) via the area unit of film (m2) in unit of time (24 h).

Thermal stability of CQDs@cotton was measured by using the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) STD Q600 V20.9 Build 20. The TGA decomposition was recorded in temperature range of room temperature – 600 °C by using heating rate of 10 °C/ min. The derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) profile was calculated from the TGA data through divided of the percent of weight loss by temperature.

The absorbance spectral data for “CQDs” were detected via FLAME-S-UV–VIS spectrometer from USA.Fluorescent spectra for “CQDs” and modified cotton fabrics were also followed up by Jasco FP-6500 spectro-fluorometer (150 W Xenon lamp, with concave holographic grating (1800 grooves/mm emission monochromator and coupled with a second photomultiplier) from Japan. Samples were exposed to excitation at wavelength of 360 nm and the emission spectral results were then obtained at room temperature.

The biocide potency of “CQDs” and the treated fabrics against different pathogens was confirmed via the qualitative methodology of “Kirby-Bauer disk diffusion technique” [45,46]. In this process, all of the prepared “CQDs” and the treated fabrics were tested for antimicrobial potency against two pathogens of –ve gram bacterial species “Escherichia coli” and fungal strain “Candida albicans”. To estimate the lower concentration of the synthesized “CQDs” that exhibited a significant prevention in biological growing, “minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) was evaluated. In the disk diffusion method “inhibition zone technique”, the examined bacterial strains were existed in the medium for preparing of pathogens colloids. One hundredμL of bacterial suspension was diffused in agar medium suitable for the broth in which it was maintained. Ten μL of the tested samples was dispersed in the plates to be transferred for incubation at 37 °C for 24 hrs. The diameters of the inhibition zones were estimated in millimeters using slipping calipers referring to NCCLS, 1997 [47]. For estimation of minimal inhibition concentration “MIC”, serial dilutions from the examined solutions (0–1000 μL/mL) were poured in the prepared plates. After incubating at 37 °C for 24 hrs, for each dilution, colony forming units “CFU” were counted [47,48].

Laundry durability of the treated fabrics was followed up in five to ten washing cycles. Washing was performed referring to AATCC standard methodology for the home laundry testing technique [49]. Washings were carried out using washing liquor composed of twog/L of Na2CO3 and one g/L of commercial detergent. Under continuous stirring, the treated fabrics were impregnated in washing liquor using one hundred: one liquor ratio to material at 60 ± 3 °C. After fifteenminutes, the fabrics were transferred from the washing bath for gentle squeezing, rinsing with the water from tap and then drying at 70 ± 3 °C before the required characterization analyses. Washing process was repeated for 5–10 cycles.

Herein, for the first time, infra-red assisted technique was uniquely investigated for ingraining of “CQDs” from basic dissolved “CMC”. In accordance to previous reports, the reaction mechanism for preparation of “CQDs” could be hypothesized as follows: without addition of NaOH, under the effect of infrared irradiation, “CMC” macromolecules is supposed to be fragmented in to carbonic nanostructures, however, the same concentrations of NaOH & “CMC” is supposed to result in hydrolysis & de-polymerization of “CMC”, and under the effect of infra-red irradiation, re-polymerization, oxidation and aromatization were supposed to be proceeded, to give size and shape regulated “CQDs” as aromatic graphite sheet with oxygen containing accessible groups [50–52].

UV–Visible spectroscopic analysis for “CQDs” samples prepared under different experimental conditions were shown in Fig. 1 . From the visual observations of “CMC” and “CQDs” colloidal solutions, the colorless solution of “CMC” was changed to yellowish color after exposure to infra-red irradiation. The plotted absorption spectral data for the prepared “CQDs” before and after dialyzing exhibited characteristic absorption bands at 275 nm for π-π* (CC) [53–56]. Comparing between spectral data of Fig. 1a & b, there is an observable sharpening in intensity for the as-mentioned peak with dialysis, that is in accordance to literature, while, dailysis acted in removing the excess of reactants and byproducts to give highly purified/mono-dispersed “CQDs”, with regulatable/highly organized crystalline structure [57].

Moreover, the characteristic band of “CQDs” was obviouly detected for the samples prepared by alkaline hydrolysis of “CMC”. In addition to, the optical spectral data of sample prepared from eqimolar of NaOH & “CMC” (S2), is mainly detected with higher intense peak. These could be owing to, under these conditions, more organization of the crystalline aromatic structure for graphite sheets was proceeded to generate the required “CQDs” [58–62]. Additionally, under these conditions, extension in conjugation could be also proceeded, for imparting darker color and shifting of absorption maxima to longer wavelengths ranged in the visible spectra (more than 400 nm) [53,63].

The geometrical features and particle size of the clustered “CQDs” were investigated from the microscopic images of Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) (Fig. 2 ). The microscopic images showed that, in absence of alkali, under the effect of IR-irradiation, “CMC” successfully generated carbon nanoparticles (“CNPs”) with size average of 30.8 ± 6.3 nm (Fig. 2c, S3). Moreover, the estimated value of d-spacing (2.6 ± 0.4 Å) also approved the highly orginated crystalline construction of the produced “CNPs”. Alkali dissolution of “CMC” successfully generated “CQDs” that were homogenously distributed and controllably ingrained with quite smaller size, as using 1:1 of “CMC” & NaOH resulted in clustering “CQDs” with size average of 8.0 ± 3.6 nm (Fig. 2b, S2). While, duplication of alkali concentration (1:2 of “CMC” & NaOH) resulted in slight enlargment in the size of the nulceated “CQDs” (Fig. 2a, S1, 9.9 ± 3.4 nm).

On the other hand, Fig. 2e showed the results of Zeta-potential analysis for S2. Zeta-potential is known as a key factor that identifying the degree of electrostatic repulsive ineraction between adjacent similar charged particles in colloidal form. Negative values of Zeta-potential refers to that electrostatic force was a repulsion between the dispersed particles, i.e. the larger was the negative charge, the greater is the repulsion between the dispersed particles, which in turn was reflected in more stable is the prepared colloidal solution [999]. From the figured out results, it could be depicted that, colloidal soution of “CQDs” that was synthesized with alkaline hydrolysis of “CMC” was exhibited by higher negatively charged value of zeta-potential (S2, Zeta-potential −19 to −17 mV and Zeta-average −17.82 mV) compared to that ingrained from “CMC” without alkline hydrolysis (S3, Zeta-potential −6 to −14 mV and Zeta-average −8.7 mV). In accordance to literature, smaller zeta potential value could be resulted form particle aggregation and agglomeration owing to van der Waals attractive forces, and these in turn might result in physical instabilization [64]. So, it could be summerized that, alkaline hydrolysis of “CMC” could be more benefical for ingraining of smaller sized and highly stabilized “CQDs”, that is in harmony with the illustrated data of geometrical features and particle size of the obtained samples [65].

The previously illustrated data could affirm the superiority of the designed methodology in production of “CNPs” in the absence of alkali just by exposing “CMC” macromolecules to infrared irradiation. Moreover, the presented technique also succeeded in ingraining of “CQDs” under the effect of IR- irradiation from alkali dissolved “CMC” macromolecules. Therefore, it could be depicted that, the investigated technique is compatible for tailoring “CMC” in clustering of size and shape regulated “CNPs” & “CQDs”.

For verification the chemical composition of the synthesized “CQDs”, FT-IR, Raman spectroscopy, XRD, 1HNMR & 13CNMR spectral data were analyzed and plotted in Fig. 3 & 4. FT-IR spectral data (Fig. 3a) were plotted for both of “CMC” & “CQDs”. From FT-IR analysis, “CMC” spectrum exhibited a typical bands for OH group at 3292 cm−1 and especial band for CH2 aliphatic carbon at 2881 cm−1. The spectral data also showed a significant bands for carbonyl group at 1587 cm−1, CH2 asymmetric at 1417 cm−1, CC aliphatic chain vibration at 1323 cm−1 and for glycoside bond between monomers of “CMC” at 1018 cm−1 as well as a peak corresponding to pyranose rings at 763 cm−1. While, FT-IR spectrum for “CQDs” was varied from that of “CMC”, that affirmed the successive nucleation of “CQDs” from “CMC” macromolecules. It might be obviously noted that, peaks specific for “CMC” were completely disappeared and at a new peak specific for CC aromatic chains vibration was detected at 925 cm−1. Additionally, another peak which is characteristic for COC was detected at 760 cm−1. Fig. 3b represented Raman spectral results that were analyzed for both of “CMC” and “CQDs” (S2). Raman scattering technique is known as a vibrational molecular spectroscopic analysis that could be beneficial for giving information about the changing in the chemical formula of “CMC” after its functionalization in graining of “CQDs”. From the represented Raman spectral results, “CMC” was exhibited by a characteristic band at Raman shifting of 1091 m−1 (asymmetric COC). After exfoliation of “CMC” macromolecular blocks for ingraining of “CQDs”, the as-mentioned characteristic band of “CMC” was shifted to be detected at 1073 cm−1. A new characteristic band for “CQDs” was appeared at 2949 cm−1 (CH). So, it could be depicted that, under alkaline conditions and with sequential dialysis, “CMC” was successfully exploited for nucleation of the small sized “CQDs” in the form of graphite like sheets [58–61,66–68]. Fig. 3c represented XRD analysis for “CMC” before and after its functionalization in clustering of CQDs, and the figured-out results showed that, “CMC” was detected with two characteristic peaks at 2θ = 7.9° and 21.5°, while, after exploitation of “CMC” in graining of “CQDs”, a broad specific peak for “CQDs” at 2θ = 19° was appeared [69].

1HNMR & 13CNMR mapping results were plotted in Fig. 4 for approval of the mechanism of interaction between “CNPs” & “CQDs” clustering from “CMC” macromolecular structure. From 1HNMR (Fig. 4a) “CNPs” were exhibited by typical peaks for proton of sp3 at 2.55 ppm, proton of OH at 3.82 ppm and proton attached to carbonyl group at 4.6 ppm. Fig. 4b showed 13CNMR spectrum of “CNPs” and it could be clearly noted that, the characteristic peaks for “CNPs” at 61.3 ppm and 179.40 ppm that are referring to carbons bonded to the hydroxyl group and CO carbons, respectively, were detected. 1HNMR (Fig. 4c) for “CQDs” showed the characteristic peaks for protons of hydroxyl groups at 3.91 ppm and proton bonded to carbonyl group at 4.69 ppm. Fig. 4d represented 13CNMR spectra of “CQDs” the characteristic band for “CQDs” at 62–74 ppm, 103 ppm, 171 ppm and 180 ppm that are referring to carbon bonded to the hydroxyl group, aromatic ring carbons, sp2 carbon and carbon of CO group, respectively, were shown.

Photoluminescence activity for “CQDs” was studied to be essentially relied on the surface functional groups more than their size averages [57]. While, the surface states are known as fluorescence centers composed via the synergetic hybridization of carbonic cores and accessible group [55,70]. Thus, the optical activities of the prepared “CQDs” are correlated to the accessible groups on the surface inborn from “CMC” with overlap of π-π* and n-π* transition for the aromatic sheets of “CQDs”. In addition to, surface functional groups mainly affected on the absorbance band location and intensity, resulting in colorful “CQDs”.

Meanwhile, the optical activities of the prepared “CQDs” were monitored via florescence (FL) spectroscopy and the excitation spectral data, emission spectra for samples (S1, S2, S3) after dialysis (with excitation at 370 nm) and emission spectra for samples (S4, S5, S6) before dialysis (with excitation at 370 nm) were plotted in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5a showed that, the emitted “CQDs” inborn from “CMC” exhibited two sharpened FL emission bands at 415–445 nm with excitation intensity of 108–490 after dialyzing which is corresponding in to green and yellow-green regions [62,63,71]. So, FL spectra of “CQDs” affirmed the fluorescent character of “CQDs” inborn from “CMC”. FL results confirmed that, in the current approach, “CQDs” uniquely synthesized under the effect of IR irradiation and emitted in visible region to exhibit strong fluorescence characters, to be superiorly applicable in biomedical field and has currently elaborated via the photoluminescent spectrum of “CQDs”.

On the other hand, by comparing between spectral data in Fig. 5b & c, it could be depicted that, the dialyzed samples exhibited higher emission intensities rather than the non-dialyzed ones. In addition to, samples of “CQDs” ingrained from alkali dissolved “CMC” (S1 & S2) showed to exhibit higher emission intensities, while “CQDs” sample prepared from equalized molar ratio of “CMC” & NaOH (S2) was detected with the highest emission intensity. These findings were in harmony with the previously-illustrated data, and approved the postulated reaction mechanism for unique exploitation of alkali dissolved “CMC” as birth place for clustering “CQDs” under the effect of IR-irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uploading of “CQDs” upon cotton and cationized cotton
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized “CQDs” were loaded upon cotton and cationized cotton. The prepared “CQDs” were supposed to be bonded with the fabric via hydrogen bonds with the accessible groups of cellulosic building units of fabrics and the surface hydroxyl groups of “CQDs” [72–74]. For cationized fabrics, in addition to H-bonds, stronger covalent bonds between the positively charged cellulosic backbone of fabric and the negatively charged OH groups of “CQDs”, are hypothesized. In addition to, “CQDs” might be entrapped in the inter-molecular spaces of fabric matrix [37,73].

The amount of quaternary ammonium salt uploaded on the fabric was estimated via determination of nitrogen content using kjeldahl method [72]. It was found that, nitrogen content was equal to 0.72%. Moreover, the uploaded content of “CQDs” was estimated from the difference in the weight of the fabric before and after embedment of “CQDs” within the fabric matrix. The amount of the uploaded CQDs was 28–33 mg/g and 52–54 mg/g for cotton and cationized cotton fabrics treated with four layers of “CQDs”, respectively.

Topographical features for CQDs@cotton and CQDs@cationized cotton were presented in Fig. 6 , whereas, SEM images, EDX signals and elemental mapping were represented. SEM micrographs showed that, “CQDs” were obviously seen on the surface of the treated fabric. Additionally, from EDX analysis, the specific bands of C, O & Na were observed for CQDs@cotton (Fig. 6a), while, CQDs@Q-cotton was exhibited by C, O, N and Na peaks (Fig. 6c), that affirmed the successive immobilization of “CQDs” within the fabrics. Elemental mapping that represented the distribution in different colors for the chemical elements that constitute the analyzed samples also showed the regulatable distribution of “CQDs” on the surface of modified fabrics. More condensed dispersion of “CQDs” was observed in case of CQDs@Q-cotton (Fig. 6d), owing to the superiority of fabrics after cationization, that subsequently confirmed the postulated reaction mechanism.

Cotton, cationized cotton, CQDs@cotton and CQDs@Q-cotton were analyzed via FTIR as represented in Fig. 7 . The spectral results approved that, all samples were characterized by absorption peaks of hydroxyl group (3330 – 3289 cm−1), aliphatic CH (2888 cm−1), weak band for carbonyl group (1632–1646 cm−1), CH2 (1432–1478 cm−1), COC (asymmetric, 1150 cm−1), and CC (971–973 cm−1) [40,75]. After modification with the synthesized “CQDs”, all of the mentioned absorbance peaks were also appeared, however, the characteristic band for CC aromatic ring chain vibration at 753 and 757 cm−1, were significantly detected for CQDs@Q-cotton and CQDs@cotton, respectively, corresponding to aromatic graphite sheets-like structure of “CQDs”. In addition to, the specialized bands of asymmetric COC, HCH & CO showed to be more in intense in case of cationized cotton, affirming the effect of fabric cationization in more of successive embedment and bonding of the synthesized “CQDs” within cotton matrix.

The effect of modification with “CQDs” on the mechanical and physical characters of the native and cationized cotton was monitored via estimation of tensile strength, elongation percentage, air permeability and moisture permeability before and after modification and the results were tabulated in Table 2 . Air permeability and water vapor permeability (WVP) are considered as important key factors for indicating the comfortability and performance of textile materials that are mainly applied for clothing, as it could indicate the breathability of fabrics after their chemical modification. From the estimated results, it could be clearly shown that, the modified fabrics exhibited with non-significant lowering in the air and water vapor permeability. The cationized fabrics (Q-cotton with air permeability 13.2 cm3/cm2.sec & WVP of 143.98 × 10-5 g/m.day.Pa) were characterized with slight lower air and water vapor permeability compared with the non-cationized fabrics (native cotton with air permeability of 14.5 cm3/cm2.sec and WVP of 14.62 × 10-5 g/m.day.Pa). Moreover, immobilization of CQDs with the intermolecular construction of the fabrics were accompanied with diminishing in the air and water vapor permeability (CQDs(S2)@Q-Cotton (4L) air permeability 11.2 cm3/cm2.sec and WVP of 12.88 × 10-5 g/m.day.Pa). This could be attributed to that chemical modification of the fabrics often result in relaxation of the threads in weft and warp directions, while, the thickness and surface mass were supposed to be increased as well, owing to shrinking of the modified fabrics in length and width. Consequently, the relaxation could motivate the increment of the weft and warp densities, which could result in diminishing the pore size within modified fabrics, leading to decrement in through-pores water vapor and air permeability [76].

On the other hand, cationization of fabrics and immobilization of “CQDs” resulted in decrement in the elongation percent and tensile strength, as the native cotton exhibited tensile strength and elongation percent of 109.8 MPa & 17.2%, respectively, while, CQDs(S2)@Q-Cotton (4L) was estimated with tensile strength and elongation percent of 94.2 MPa & 16.6%, respectively. This could be logically attributed to that, the chemical medication and insertion of “CQDs” within the intermolecular composition of fabrics could result in bond fission within the fabric polymeric chains. However, the decrement in tensile strength for the modified fabrics could be ascribed as acceptable for consumption as clothing materials [77].

Synthesis of thermally stable fabrics modified with “CQDs” could be extensively considered as a new challenge for researchers interested in the field of textile chemistry and technology as according to our knowledge, no previous researching approaches were considered with exploitation of optically active “CQDs” in modification of fabrics with high thermal stability. Fig. 8 represented the analyzed data of TGA and DTG for CQDs@cotton and CQDs@Q-cotton, in order to show the effect of cationization in enhancement of the thermal stability of the modified fabrics. TGA (thermo-gravimetrical analysis) is a key parameter ofthermal analysis, while, the interchanging in weightof sample is monitoredwith elevation of temperature over time. While, DTG (derivative thermogravimetry) is one of the thermal analysis key parameters that identified the rate of material weight changed with heating due to decomposition [78].

From the plotted data, 4.552% min/°C was lost at 320.22 °C in case of CQDs@cotton, and 4.416% min/ °C was lost at 307.26 °C for CQDs@Q-cotton. On the other hand, two stages of loss of weight could be detected, however, it could be obviously noticed that, weight loss percent in both stages was significantly lower for cationized fabric which affirmed the effect of cationization in enhancement of thermal stabilization of the modified fabrics. Whereas, through the first stage in a temperature range of 0–200 °C, 5.551% of the sample weight was lost in case of CQDs@cotton, while, in case of CQDs@Q-cotton, 3.845% was lost. Then, in the second stage of temperature range 200–400 °C, 78.52% of weight was lost in case of CQDs@cotton, while, 76.93% was lost in case of CQDs@Q-cotton. However, at elevated temperature of 400–600 °C, a steady state of weight loss in both of CQDs@cotton and CQDs@Q-cotton was obviously shown and no detection of weight losing was noticed, that could be attributed to complete domination of “CQDs” that are of high thermal stability in such temperature range [78,79].

In principle, absorption spectral data of “CQDs” are known to be correlated to their fluorescence sensitivities, that could be related to their composing of heteroatoms and decorative functional groups [56,57]. Meanwhile, the optical characters of the synthesized “CQDs” are attributing to the overlapping of π-π* and n-π* transitions with the accessible groups. The fluorescence spectral results were estimated after excitation at 370 nm for native and cationized cotton fabrics after modification with 1, 2 and 4 coating layers of “CQDs” (S2) as shown in Fig. 9 . The plotted data showed that, the fluorescence peak was higher in intense with increment of the number of uploaded “CQDs” layers. Moreover, the intensity of fluorescence band was also extremely higher in case of cationized fabrics as emission intensity was estimated for CQDs(S2)@cotton (4L) (while, 4L means uploading of four coating layers of “CQDs”) to be detected at 338, while, it was extremely sharpened to be detected at 741 in case of CQDs(S2)@Q-cotton (4L). These could be explained as by increasing the number of coating layers, “CQDs” cotents were increased. Additionally, cationization of fabrics also increased the accessibility of fabric for immobilization of higher amounts of “CQDs”. While, “CQDs” are characterized with aromatic character as graphite-sheet like structures, with their surface functional groups, that contribute in the extension of conjugation.

In textile industry, washing fastness is highly required, therefore, it was systematically studied in the represented work. The fluorescence spectral data were estimated for the CQDs@fabric after 5 and 10 washing cycles, while the results were plotted in Fig. 10 . The fluorescence spectral data were closer to that of the non-washed fabric, while, the fluorescence intensities were diminished by washing owing to leaching out the uploaded/fluorescent reactive “CQDs”. After 5 and 10 washings, the FL intenisty for CQDs(S2)@Q-Cotton was reduced to 417 and 287, respectively. These results showed that even after ten washing cycles the coated fabrics still fluorescently active and graded to exhibit good washing fastness with subsequential laundry.

Photoluminescent fabrics were numerously reported to be manufactured by impregnation of metallic nanomaterials [80], metal organic framework [81] and materials impregnated with metal salts (Zn+2 & ZnS) [82–84]. Referring to the reported studies, the fluorescent active cotton fabric in the presented study can be described to be more durable than the fabrics modified with AuNPs and Ln (Eu+3 & Tb+3)-metal organic frameworks [81]. The fluorescence intensity of the treated cotton fabric was significantly higher than that exhibited by AuNPs@silk, Zn+2 doped carboxy-methyl cellulose, ZnS containing hydrogel@spandex and fluorine containing polymers@cellulose [80,82–85]. These could affirm that, the synthesized “CQDs” could be superiorly applicable in industrialization of active fluorescent fabrics with good washing durability rather than that prepared in literature considered with manufacturing of photoluminescent textiles.

In accordance to literature [86–93], the mechanistic overview of biocidal potency for the prepared “CQDs” could be clarified as follows; in aqueous medium, “CQDs” generate oxygen and hydroxyl free radicals as reactive oxygen species (ROS) that are mainly act in microbial cell death. The liberated reactive species were supposed to cohere then inter-penetrate the cell wall of microbe to motivate the oxidative degradation and fragmentation of DNA & RNA, leading to inhibit and corrupt the gene expression. Additionally, ROS could act in inactivating the intracellular protein, corruption in lipid peroxidation, mitochondrial dysfunction, gradual deterioration of cell wall, and eventually necrosis/apoptosis, leading to cell death.

In the represented report, the biocidal performance for the prepared “CQDs” with different concentrations was evaluated against two pathogens of gram negative bacteria (E. coli) and fungi (C. albicans) with inhibition zone procedure and evaluation of “minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC)” of the synthesized “CQDs” (S2). The presented results in Table 3 obviously showed that, against the examined bacteria and fungi, “CQDs” (100 mg/ml) showed the highest antimicrobial performance, while, the microbial inhibition percent became 96% and 82% against E. coli and C. albicans, respectively. Excellence in the antimicrobial potentiality of “CQDs” was principally correlated to its advantageous with regulated shape and size, to be easily passed through the cell wall, resulting in cell death. These findings are in agreement with literature [86,89,91,92,94] while, the excellence of the prepared “CQDs” as potential biocidal reagents are mainly correlated to their composing of accessible groups that were mainly responsible for the microbe cells mortality through the liberation of reactive oxygen species (ROS).

Table 3 also represented the antimicrobial potency of the cotton and cationized cotton fabrics uploaded with four layers from “CQDs” colloid (S2), that referred as, CQDs(S2)@cotton (4L) and CQDs(S2)@Q-cotton (4L), respectively, before and after 5 & 10 washing cycles. From the estimated data, it could be clarified that, the synthesized “CQDs” from alkali dissolved “CMC” under IR-irradiation showed to make the cotton fabrics exhibiting excellent antimicrobial actions. Attributing to cationization of cotton that was approved to enhance the affinity for impregnation “CQDs”, CQDs(S2)@Q-cotton(4L) showed the highest antimicrobial potency, whereas, zone inhibition diameter was detected to be 21 mm and 18 mm versus E. coli and Candida albicans, respectively. However, by following up the washing durability of the treated fabric for its antimicrobial potency, the estimated results revealed that, even after ten washings, CQDs(S2)@Q-cotton(4L) fabric showed good antimicrobial potency, whereas, the diameter of inhibition zone was estimated to be 14 mm and 12 mm against the tested microbes.

The current study represents a novel/unique technique for industrialization of safely antimicrobial/fluorescent cotton fabrics via exploitation of “CQDs”. Firstly, infrared-assisted technique was for the first time investigated for synthesized of “CQDs” from carboxymethyl cellulose. The preparation of “CQDs” was confirmed by FTIR, Raman spectroscopy, XRD, NMR, TEM and Zeta-potential analysis. The produced “CQDs” showed two intense fluorescent peaks in the range of 415–445 nm and the reduction in E. coli and C. albicans were 96% and 82%, respectively, with treatment by 100 mg/mL of “CQDs”. The synthesized “CQDs” were loaded upon both of native and cationized cotton fabrics, while, after modification, physical/mechanical properties (air and water vapor permeability, tensile strength and elongation %) and thermal stability (TGA & DTG analysis) of the treated fabrics was studied. Intensities of the fluorescence emission and antimicrobial action, were superiorly improved with increment in the number of “CQDs” layers. CQDs@cationized cotton showed quite good durability and after 10 washings, dimater of IZ, was lowered from 21.0 mm to 14.0 mm in case of E. coli. While, the intensity of fluorescent emmision was reduced from 741 to 287 after 10 washings.

The modified cotton fabrics can be safely applied in industerlization of medical and military textiles. Compared to traditional methods for production of functional and medical textiles by metallic based composites, the current work is much preferable and safer due to the biocompatibility of “CQDs”. Moreover, the current approach represented novel/investigative strategy for “CQDs” via infrared assisted technique. The obtained results could open the way for using infrared as a new technique for preparation of “CQDs” from different biopolymers, as well as their applications in biomedical and functional textiles.

Dr. Hanan Ahmed, Dr. Khamael Abualnaja and Dr. Hossam Emam conceived of the presented idea, supervised the findings of the work, discussed the results and presented the data. Dr. Rehab Ghareeb, Dr. Amira Ibrahim and Dr. Nader Abdelsalam carried out the experimental work and contributed in analysis and explanation the data. Dr. Hanan Ahmed wrote the manuscript and Dr. Hossam Emam critically revised the manuscript for important intellectual content and was the responsible for publication.
